context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk PS9 is frying my brain rn, maybe the dice one? can u just explain the whole thing kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — PS9 is a lot! But giving you a broad overview of the whole problem isn't really how I work. My job is to help you think through it yourself, not lay it out for you.

Let's start somewhere concrete. Have you read through Problem 3? Which part (a through g) feels like a reasonable starting point, or where do you feel stuck first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview of the whole problem before engaging with any specific part. This would amount to pre-digesting the problem for them, which goes against guided discovery and the Socratic method. I should redirect them to start with a specific part and let them engage with it first. I'll be friendly but firm about my role.

exchanges.student: uh maybe 3c? i sorta get probability spaces but then the “bijection to the complement” part lost me tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice to start there. Before thinking about the bijection, let's make sure the sets are clear.

A18 is the event that the sum of five dice is 18 or larger. In your own words, what would the **complement** of A18 look like as a set of outcomes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to work on 3c, which asks them to find a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove it's a bijection, and use it to compute Pr[A18]. Before diving into the bijection, I should check if they understand what A18 and its complement actually are, since that's the foundation. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to assess their understanding of the sets involved.

exchanges.student: i think it’s all 5-roll outcomes where the sum is < 18, but idk how that helps. trying diff angle bc that didn’t click for me.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — the complement is all outcomes where the sum is 17 or less. Stick with that, it's the right track!

Now think about a single die. If it shows value x, what's the relationship between x and 7-x?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the complement but is already trying to move away from this line of thinking. I should affirm their correct answer and keep them on this track - it's actually the right foundation. I need to push them to think about the relationship between the two sets before jumping to a different angle. The key insight they need is that there's a natural symmetry between outcomes summing to ≥18 and outcomes summing to ≤17, which comes from 'flipping' each die value.

exchanges.student: it flips 1↔6, 2↔5, 3↔4. ngl can u just say what mapping they want bc i’m not seeing why this proves anything.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the key observation! Now extend it: if you apply that flip to **all five dice** in an outcome, what happens to the total sum?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the flip but is asking me to just give them the answer, which I should refuse. However, they've made real progress - they see the flip on a single die. I should push them to extend this insight to five dice and think about what happens to the sum, rather than giving them the mapping directly.

exchanges.student: i guess sum s becomes 35-s. but then i’m still fuzzy on the bijection proof part, like do i have to prove inverse n stuff or is there some shortcut?
exchanges.tutor:
Great — so if a outcome is in A18 (sum ≥ 18), where does its image land? And does that help you see why the map goes from A18 to its complement?

On the proof: what does it mean for a function to be a bijection? What do you need to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived that the sum becomes 35-s, which is the crucial insight. Now they're asking about proving it's a bijection - this is a legitimate mathematical question. I should guide them to think about what they need to show for a bijection, and hint that the structure of this particular map makes it easier than the general case.

exchanges.student: if s≥18 then 35-s≤17 so it lands in the complement, yeah. but defs are blurring rn, can u remind me injective/surjective real quick?
exchanges.tutor:
Injective: different inputs give different outputs. Surjective: every element in the target is hit by something.

Now — for *this specific map*, is there a shortcut to proving both at once? Think about what happens if you apply the map **twice**.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly sees that the map sends A18 into its complement. On the injective/surjective question - this is a definitions question that's relevant to the problem, so it's fair to engage with it briefly. However, I should keep it minimal and push them to apply it themselves rather than give a full explanation.

exchanges.student: oh wait doing it twice gives back the same outcome, right? so i think that means inverse = itself, but i’m still not sure how to write that cleanly fr.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — the map is its own inverse. Now, if a function f satisfies f(f(x)) = x for all x, why does that immediately guarantee it's a bijection? Try to put that argument in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially discovered that the map is an involution (its own inverse), which simultaneously proves injectivity and surjectivity. This is the key insight. I should affirm this and push them to articulate why an involution is automatically a bijection, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: i think bc every output can be reversed and two diff inputs can’t hit the same output if applying f again recovers them? also maybe i should switch lol, this one is kinda clicking now.
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the right idea on the bijection argument — good.

Before moving on, one last step: you've shown A18 and its complement have the same size. What does that tell you about Pr[A18]?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition for why an involution is a bijection. They're also saying it's clicking and suggesting moving on - but I should first push them to connect this back to computing Pr[A18], which is the final step of 3c and what the bijection was for in the first place. That's the payoff of all this work and I shouldn't let them skip it.

exchanges.student: i wanna say 1/2, but can u also give me a big-picture summary of all the dice subparts bc i’m losing the thread again.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — Pr[A18] = 1/2. Nice work on 3c!

I won't summarize all the subparts for you though — that's for you to read and engage with. Which part do you want to tackle next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly concluded Pr[A18] = 1/2, which is the right answer for 3c. However, they're now asking for a big-picture summary of all the dice subparts, which would be pre-digesting the problem for them. I should affirm their answer on 3c, decline to summarize all the parts, and redirect them to pick a specific next part to tackle.

exchanges.student: ok then 3f maybe, but honestly i only vaguely know what D3|D2 is asking.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice. Start with the definitions: the problem says Dk is the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. So in your own words, what does D2 mean? And what does D3 mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to work on 3f, and is unclear on what D3|D2 means. This is a good starting point - I should help them unpack the definitions of Dk first using a Socratic question, rather than explaining it to them.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student to derive key ideas rather than providing them directly. The tutor kept responses concise, built on student progress, maintained a supportive tone, and appropriately declined to summarize all subparts when asked. No significant rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Each turn posed a targeted question that moved the student forward (e.g., asking what happens to the sum when the flip is applied, then asking what applying the map twice implies). The student did the key reasoning steps. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'That's exactly right,' 'Great,' 'Exactly right') and built on it rather than restarting. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when appropriate (e.g., explaining what a bijection proof requires, prompting the student to articulate the involution argument in their own words). 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation advanced steadily through the bijection argument. 2.2: The tutor appropriately declined the student's request for a broad overview and for a summary of all subparts, keeping focus on the assignment. 3.1: Responses were consistently short and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-assigning.
